stem: You build a circuit and set `qc.global_phase = np.pi / 4` (no other change), then sample it many times on a simulator. How does the global phase affect the measured bitstring counts?
code:
import numpy as np
from qiskit import QuantumCircuit

qc = QuantumCircuit(1)
qc.h(0)
qc.global_phase = np.pi / 4   # multiplies the whole statevector by e^{i pi/4}
qc.measure_all()

options.text: It raises an error at run time, because a global phase leaves the state un-normalized
Not at all — a global phase multiplies the entire statevector by e^{iπ/4}, which is physically unobservable, so every measurement probability is unchanged
The relative frequencies shift toward outcome 1, because the phase adds amplitude to |1⟩
It rotates the qubit by π/4, changing the measurement probabilities like an RY(π/4) would

explanation.correct: A global phase multiplies every amplitude by the same unit-modulus factor e^{iθ}. Since measurement probabilities are |amplitude|², the common factor cancels, so the counts are statistically identical to the phase-free circuit. Global phase is physically unobservable.

distractors.C: A global phase multiplies ALL amplitudes equally; it does not preferentially add weight to |1⟩. Relative frequencies are unchanged.
distractors.D: `global_phase` is a scalar factor on the whole state, not a single-qubit rotation. Unlike RY(π/4), it moves no population between basis states.
distractors.A: e^{iπ/4} has modulus 1, so the state stays normalized; setting `global_phase` is valid and raises no error.
